journal_entry.description: Factory and office electricity charges paid by corporate credit card
journal_entry.rationale: Factory electricity ($15,000) is a manufacturing overhead cost that should be capitalized to inventory or expensed as cost of goods sold depending on production activity. Office electricity ($5,500) is an administrative expense. Both are paid via corporate credit card, creating a liability. The transaction text clearly separates factory vs office charges, allowing proper classification. Factory electricity is recorded as Manufacturing Overhead (or Factory Utilities) which will flow through cost of goods sold, while office electricity is an operating expense.

output.reason: Factory electricity ($15,000) = manufacturing overhead expense increase (cost of production). Office electricity ($5,500) = administrative/operating expense increase. These are two distinct expense categories requiring separate debit lines, both increasing expense accounts.

debit_classifier.reason: Factory electricity ($15,000) = manufacturing overhead expense increase (cost of production). Office electricity ($5,500) = administrative/operating expense increase. These are two distinct expense categories requiring separate debit lines, both increasing expense accounts.

output.reason: Payment made using corporate credit card creates a liability increase (credit card payable). The factory electricity ($15,000) and office electricity ($5,500) are expenses being incurred, not decreased. The credit side records only the credit card liability increase.

credit_classifier.reason: Payment made using corporate credit card creates a liability increase (credit card payable). The factory electricity ($15,000) and office electricity ($5,500) are expenses being incurred, not decreased. The credit side records only the credit card liability increase.
